sections.heading: Simple Reflex Agent
sections.body: A simple reflex agent selects actions based only on the current percept, ignoring the rest of the percept history, using condition-action rules like "if X then do Y." It's fast and simple but only works well in fully observable environments where the current percept contains everything needed to decide.

sections.heading: Model-Based Agent
sections.body: A model-based reflex agent maintains an internal state that tracks aspects of the world it can't currently observe, updated based on percept history and knowledge of how the world evolves. This lets it handle partially observable environments that simple reflex agents can't.

sections.heading: Goal-Based Agent
sections.body: A goal-based agent acts to achieve explicit goals, not just react to the current state. It considers the future consequences of actions — essentially asking "will this get me closer to my goal?" — which requires reasoning about sequences of actions, not just single-step reactions.

sections.heading: Utility-Based Agent
sections.body: A utility-based agent goes further than a goal-based one by using a utility function that assigns a numeric "happiness" score to different world states, allowing it to choose among multiple ways of reaching a goal (or trade off conflicting goals) by picking the option with the highest expected utility.

sections.heading: Learning Agent
sections.body: A learning agent can improve its own performance over time. It's typically described with four conceptual components: a performance element (decides actions), a learning element (makes improvements based on feedback), a critic (evaluates how well the agent is doing against a fixed performance standard), and a problem generator (suggests exploratory actions to discover better strategies).

quiz.question: A simple reflex agent decides its action based on:
options: Only the current percept, via condition-action rules

quiz.question: What distinguishes a model-based agent from a simple reflex agent?
options: It has no rules at all
It maintains internal state to handle partial observability
It only works in fully observable environments

quiz.question: Which agent type uses a numeric function to compare and trade off different outcomes?
